title: <div><tt>The hunter’s casual offer of a nickel, and Phoenix’s eventual decision
content:
The hunter’s casual offer of a nickel, and Phoenix’s eventual decision to accept it, adds to the racial dynamics at play. While her acceptance may seem like a small, pragmatic act, it also underscores the complicated relationship between African Americans and the white community during the time. Phoenix accepts the nickel, but not without a sense of dignity, acknowledging both the need for survival and the transactional nature of such encounters. This moment reinforces the tension between the need for survival and the dignity Phoenix maintains in the face of racial condescension.

from Week 5 Discussion
Feb 9, 2025 1:38PM
Michael Perez
Hi Karima, thank you for sharing your take on the symbolism displayed in the story, “The Worn Path”. In my opinion the hunter does in fact symbolize racial tension. Theres a point in the story when the hunter points his gun at Phoenix, I believe it not only serves to evoke fear but also reinforces the power struggle between white and black people during that time period. His actions point to a broader systemic norm that sought to limit the movement and autonomy of black people. However, as you pointed out, despite his open threats, Phoenix continues to stand her ground and faces him "straight-faced."(Welty, 1941) Her bold refusal to be intimidated by the hunter, even in the face of personal harm to herself and/or her own life, is a testament to her inner strength and her resilience in the face of oppression. It would also appear to serve as a stark example of just how profoundly terrifying it must have been for people of color and the lengths; they had to resort to for the simplest of basic lifesaving necessities. For Phoenix to have not even bat an eye at his extreme aggression and the belligerent manner in which he approached her again displays how typical this l
